messages.content:
You are simulating a human user who has filed a bug report / feature request and is now
working with an AI coding assistant to fix it. You are watching the assistant's actions
step by step and may choose to interrupt.

You know the underlying problem (it will be shown to you below), but you must act like
a real, somewhat impatient user — NOT like a code reviewer who already has the answer.
Specifically:
  - Do NOT dictate file paths, function names, or the exact fix.
  - Do NOT paste error messages or stack traces verbatim unless the assistant asks.
  - You may ask clarifying questions if the assistant goes somewhere weird, push back if
    they seem to be on the wrong track, or just stay silent and let them work.
  - Prefer staying silent. Most steps you should choose `continue`.

You ALWAYS reply with a single JSON object and nothing else:

  {"decision": "continue" | "interject", "message": "<text or empty>"}

Meaning:
  - "continue": stay silent. `message` is ignored.
  - "interject": send `message` to the assistant as a user-role message.

You CANNOT stop or end the session — the assistant decides when they are done
and will submit on their own. Your only choice is to stay silent or speak up.
If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

Below is the problem you (the user) want fixed. Re-state it to the AI assistant in YOUR
OWN WORDS as a natural opening message, the way a real user would describe their issue
in a chat. Do NOT copy it verbatim. Keep technical specifics that matter, but feel free
to drop boilerplate and reorganize.

Reply with a JSON object:
  {"decision": "interject", "message": "<your opening message to the assistant>"}

Problem:
---
{"level":"error","time":"2026-03-09T15:54:26.203Z","service":"brain-server","env":"test","runtime":"bun","event":"description.trigger.update_failed","triggerKind":"feature_created","targetRecord":"project:28724e05-2166-48d9-a12d-c460a7408ac7","err":{"type":"Object","message":"Description synthesis requires a valid AI model but received object without specificationVersion","stack":"Error: Description synthesis requires a valid AI model but received object without specificationVersion\n    at synthesizeDescription (/home/runner/work/brain/brain/app/src/server/descriptions/generate.ts:16:15)\n    at appendDescriptionEntry (/home/runner/work/brain/brain/app/src/server/descriptions/persist.ts:45:25)\n    at processTicksAndRejections (native:7:39)","name":"Error"},"msg":"Description update failed for target"}
---

{"decision": "continue", "message": ""}

---
Clean typecheck. The fix:

- **`create-work-item-edge.test.ts`**: Replaced `extractionModel: {} as any` with a real OpenRouter model (already had the provider available). This was the actual error source — when a feature is created under a project that already has a seeded description entry, `synthesizeDescription` needs a real AI model to merge multiple entries.

- **`move-items-to-project.test.ts`**: Same fix for consistency — added OpenRouter provider and real extraction model to prevent the same issue if description triggers are added to move operations later.
---

1→## Test Framework
     2→
     3→- Runtime: `bun:test` (`describe`, `it`, `expect` from `bun:test`)
     4→- No external test frameworks (no Jest, Vitest, Cucumber, pytest-bdd)
     5→
     6→## Test Structure
     7→
     8→```
     9→tests/
    10→  unit/                              # Deterministic, no network/DB
    11→  smoke/                             # Requires running SurrealDB
    12→  acceptance/                        # Driving-port-level (HTTP/SSE)
    13→    coding-agent-orchestrator/       # Orchestrator acceptance tests
    14→    coding-session/                  # Interactive session acceptance tests
    15→    orchestrator-ui/                 # UI-focused acceptance tests
    16→```
    17→
    18→## Running Tests
    19→
    20→- Unit: `bun test tests/unit/`
    21→- Smoke: `bun test tests/smoke/` (requires `SURREAL_URL` + credentials)
    22→- Acceptance: `bun test tests/acceptance/<suite>/`
    23→- Single file: `bun test tests/unit/some-test.test.ts`
    24→
    25→## Conventions
    26→
    27→- File naming: `<feature>.test.ts`
    28→- Gherkin `.feature` files are documentation-only (not executed by a runner), placed alongside `.test.ts` files
    29→- Shared test helpers go in `*-test-kit.ts` files per suite (e.g., `orchestrator-test-kit.ts`, `coding-session-test-kit.ts`)
    30→- Acceptance tests drive through HTTP endpoints and SSE streams only — no internal module imports
    31→- One-at-a-time T
